customer_anxiety_profile: Fixed-income customer on Social Security who self-described as 'flat broke.' Financial exposure is her primary anxiety — she screened for cost in the first 18 seconds. She is cooperative and not hostile, but her engagement is polite-passive rather than enthusiastic. The 'we don't wanna waste our time' exit framing may have introduced doubt about whether she qualifies, reducing callback pickup motivation.
unresolved_concerns: Credit score unknown — Margaret does not know her score and has never had it checked. The policy question of whether to DQ on stated credit vs. pass to closer's pull is unresolved at the floor level (see qualifiers.credit_qualifier.spencer_operator_note).
Title/co-owner status never checked — unknown whether a family member is on the title or involved in financial decisions. Must be confirmed before any appointment is set.
Bill amount is self-reported at approximately $80/month — not verified against an actual bill. Bill collection was not attempted.
sensitive_topics_raised: Fixed income / Social Security — disclosed twice unprompted
Self-described as 'flat broke' — explicit financial stress signal
Credit score unknown — potential qualifier gap depending on floor policy
exact_promises_rep_made: Parker promised a callback in approximately five minutes to report back after checking with his manager on the credit-unknown situation.
closer_should_know: Margaret is a fixed-income Social Security customer who does not know her credit score. The callback was framed as a 'we don't wanna waste our time' check — meaning Margaret may be expecting to be told she doesn't qualify. Whoever makes the callback should lead with a positive framing (e.g., 'I checked with my team and here's the path that works for you') rather than a qualification verdict. The title/co-owner question was never asked and must be addressed before any appointment is set.
